speaker: interviewer
text: This is the May 15, 1995, interview of Jenny Singer. This is tape number two. I would like to go back, just for a moment. You describe the situation where you ran away with your sister from an attic. I just was wondering what-- if you ever found out what happened to your sister-- if you ever saw her again.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, apparently she didn't go down. They didn't-- this is what I could think-- that they kept her. And they let me go. She was a beautiful young woman, and they kept her. Because later on, even after the-- later on, when I came to [NON-ENGLISH]-- can I ask if he-- have they saw my sister-- no. But then later on, my uncle sent a guy who brought money. I asked him. Maybe my uncle knows something about my sister's existence. I mean, after the liberation, nobody had any trace about my sister. So-- but-- but I assume that they used her while-- while-- I don't know-- as long as they had pleasure-- and later they killed her. Wherever I went, I left. We just went-- we have a-- a second cousin in Israel. And I wrote to him. We have relatives in Canada, in Ottawa. And I wrote to them. And I thought that my sister was older. And if I remembered the names and addresses, she also remembered. And if she would be alive, she would get in touch with them. But, so sorry-- didn't hear a thing.

speaker: interviewer
text: And then, one other thing-- you were describing the time when the Germans caught you. And they threw you into a grave. And they threw grenades on the people. And you survived. Can you just describe that a little bit more, please?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, yes. This is the same thing what occurred in November '44, and the same thing-- but this time, they took all the Jews. Nobody remained. They wanted work-- were working. It did not work. It seemed, that the rumor that my uncle came and told, eventually it did happen. He heard it all the time.

speaker: interviewee
text: But-- and there were graves. And-- all the graves were covered already, you know? And there was this one grave that wasn't covered yet, because they were still throwing in people. They caught many people, maybe like I, or maybe others, and they threw them together.

speaker: interviewee
text: And they started to cover up the grave with everything available. And it started to thunder and to lightning very strong. And they did not finish. I-- I thought because of this that they didn't finish cover-- to cover completely that grave. And they left.

speaker: interviewee
text: And what-- what we-- what I saw when I came-- clothing-- you know? They didn't even have the-- they told-- told all the people to get undressed. And the people that had gold teeth, they pulled the teeth from their mouth.

speaker: interviewee
text: Because I saw, laying in, later on, when it got so dark-- and it started to-- to thunder, you know. And they threw me in that grave too. And, I don't know. I was numb. I assumed that I'd fainted, because I-- I was not conscious. And later, I don't know even the time-- how long it-- how much time expired. And I-- I-- I heard screams and-- from-- from-- from-- from people being wounded, from people being killed, from cries. And finally, everything subsided. But I was alive. I didn't-- I didn't even think. I was numb. I was all numb. And I touched myself. And I got up. And I stood up. And I was standing. And I let the rain to wash off the blood from-- from my hair, from my head, from my body. And I saw two other girls. And we said, let's run from here before the police comes and covers the grave and discovers us. But one girl was hysterical. And she said, no. My whole family is here-- my little brother and my sisters-- and she didn't want to move. And we were not strong enough to-- to persuade her.

speaker: interviewee
text: So this-- this one girl said that she's going to go someplace to Christian people. And I went to-- to that Nadia Honovska, where I was a year and two months at the attic. When you were able to climb out of the grave, you saw two other girls?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you see anyone else alive?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. No.

speaker: interviewer
text: And were-- most of the people were killed from the hand grenades?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what-- what did they do? Toss-- the Germans tossed hand grenades in after the people were in the grave?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. They said that they didn't want to waste them later on, you know-- after the liberation. And we came. We started to discuss it. They said, they didn't want to waste bullets. And something like happened in Babi Yar and Kiev. There were 150,000 people-- mostly Jews-- were buried in Kiev.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now, when we left off, you had reached a point where you had come to some caves.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Yes?

speaker: interviewer
text: Why don't you pick up there and explain that?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. And I was in that cave and with rocks, you know, with sharp rocks. I tried to make it deeper and deeper. And I was laying there. And then I went out. One night I came to [NON-ENGLISH] and I begged for food. And she gave me some food. One night we went to Nadia Honovska now, and she brought me food. And one day, I was laying there, and I heard a dog bark. And the dog came closer, and closer. And I heard a Ukrainian speak. And he said, there must be-- probably Jews hiding. And he said, whoever is there, you don't have to come out. Just throw whatever you have. If you have gold, if you have money, just throw it, and I'll call the dog off. And then I-- I was-- I realized what my aunt met-- meant-- when she told me I should save money for an emergency. And I threw out some money. But the guy said-- whoever was there-- I assumed it was a man-- and he said, this is not enough. If I'll come in, and I'll find that you have more, I'll kill you. I'll tell the dog-- I'll-- the dog is going to-- to-- to pull you apart.

speaker: interviewee
text: And, whatever I had, I threw out. And he called off the dog. And I came out. And I came to Nadia. And I begged her, I said, what can you do for me?

speaker: interviewee
text: And this was already 1943-- almost the end of 1943. And not only did we have enemies, the Germans, the Ukrainians were our own enemies. The Poles were our enemies-- very, very few people that were really sympathetic to our cause-- very few. And that's the reason so few of us survived, like it would happen. And then the Germans came to Holland or they came to Denmark. The people-- the Danish people-- put on the Jewish stars too And they sympathized, but with us, the Poles, they gave us up. They gave us to the German authority. And this Nadia Honovska told me there was this village, a Polish village, where a certain sect of Ukrainians-- they were called [NON-ENGLISH]-- and they hated the Poles. And they killed out the whole population. It was a very small village. And the people, they used to do such horrid things-- to cut off men's penises, or women's breasts. And nobody remained alive except for a few people.

speaker: interviewee
text: So that Nadia told me the story. And she said, and the people, the few people that remained alive from that village, [NON-ENGLISH] that village-- they went to work-- to look for work. We had Czechish colonies in Poland. And she suggested I should do the same thing. She brought me a little cross. And when I was at her attic, and sometimes when I came into the house to-- to cook, I had an opportunity to study prayers, you know? And when I was sitting all by myself in there, they cannot-- I go insane. I kept repeating it to myself, to myself, that maybe one day, one day it will come in handy.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I decided to go to that village too. She brought me a long dress, a peasant dress, and an apron. And I decided to go on a little prayer book. And I decided to go to that village. It was [NON-ENGLISH], what it is called. I should-- maybe I'll get a job too. And in order to go, I had to go at night. I was afraid not be recognized.

speaker: interviewee
text: I had to cross a Christian cemetery. And it was twilight, and I got very, very panicky, you know? Somehow my imagination carried me away. And I thought that all the dead people came out from the graves and tried-- and started to chase me.

speaker: interviewee
text: And apparently, I fainted, because when it started-- getting goosebumps all over. And when I woke up, or I came to myself, there was this Polish woman standing near me. And she said-- she asked me who I am. And I told her the make-believe story.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I said that I am looking for work. And she said, are you a peasant girl too? Do you know how to milk cows? I said, yes. Do you know how to dig potatoes? Yes. Whatever she asked me, whatever I can do-- and out of fear I said, yes. So she said, OK, sit. She said, she's going to the Germans to work. And she'll come back in the afternoon. She'll take me to her place. She has one. So I stayed there.

speaker: interviewee
text: She came back. She took me. It's easy to say, but you know, I didn't know if she'll come, if she'll come with police. Maybe she-- maybe she was one of the mean ones. Sure enough, she came by herself. And she told me I should follow her. And I came to that village. And she had a little house, a little farm. She had two cows. She had four children. One daughter was my age-- six years older. One was my age, one was six years older. And the six years older was retarded. Anyway, she-- she gave me some food, and she gave me instructions that I should get up in the morning, and I should milk the cows. And do this, and do this and do this. All night I couldn't fall asleep, but you know, when you're young, nature took care. And I fell asleep. And she woke me up in the morning. She said, you know, you have to milk the cows. I am leaving. And I started to cry. Not only I knew how to-- to-- to milk cows, but I didn't-- I was afraid even to go near a cow. I was a city girl. And cows, if they're not milked after several hours, you know, they fill up with milk. And it's very painful. And they started to make so much noise-- moo. And they woke up the whole village. And this young Pole came in. He introduced himself. He said, he is the brother of this woman, Francisca [NON-ENGLISH] is the name of that woman. And he took one look at me. And he said, do not be afraid. And he said it gently-- that all the Poles used to call us [NON-ENGLISH], like-- like we would call an Afro American, negro-- that-- that's the way to call. But he, somehow, expressed himself nicely.

speaker: interviewee
text: He said, you are an Israelite, and I work together-- for the Russians, with your father. And I know you very well. You brought lunch for your father? And he said, you probably have money hidden. All Poles thought that the Jews are very rich, which is not true. And I said, yes. I told him all about the [NON-ENGLISH].

speaker: interviewee
text: I said, if you want, you go. Maybe you'll get our money-- our-- the things that we are-- have. And if not, you'll have to wait and help me survive the war. And then I'll compensate you. And he was nice enough.

speaker: interviewee
text: He taught me how to milk cows. He taught me how to dig potatoes and how to go horseback riding, and go-- go tend to fields. And I absorbed everything like a sponge, you know? Because the-- the will to live and to survive was very, very strong.

speaker: interviewee
text: And my mother always used to have hope, especially my sister, because she looks so beautiful. And she looks like an Aryan girl. And she said, you girls are blonde. And my sister had blue eyes, and I have green eyes. And she said, you must survive. You must survive. You must tell the world about all the atrocities. And I-- I thought, I'll do everything possible just to survive. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And I was going on working, working. And I worked there for a couple of weeks. And one day, that man got drunk. And he came. And he wanted to go to bed with me. And I declined.

speaker: interviewee
text: He said, see? You are too proud-- Jews. And I told him, I'm a young girl. I-- I'm not going to do things like this. He said, OK. I'm going to tell my sister, and then she'll chase you out. And sure enough, he wielded his sister. And his sister said, so what? So if she should-- but she's a maid. She does everything that four people couldn't do the work that she's doing. And let her stay.

speaker: interviewee
text: And he said, I don't think she's going to stay for too long. And another short time passed by. And he got drunk again, and he asked for the same thing. And I declined. He said to his sister, you have a choice-- either you chase her out or you are going-- I'm going to the Gestapo in [NON-ENGLISH]. They'll kill you together with-- with everybody. And you'll have everybody on your conscience.

speaker: interviewee
text: And it was already the end of 1943, and the Germans started to retreat from Stalingrad. Because I heard at night, sometimes the Poles were talking, you know? And I should not-- I didn't say anything to interfere. I didn't want them to know if I understand something. You know, I was the maid. And I had to keep quiet. But I heard. And-- and it happened that there were many deserters from the army, you know? And they circled around that village. They circled around-- all Gestapo men circled around that village. And I told her, where am I going to run now? If I will run, I'll fall right into the hands of Gestapo. And they'll-- they will find out that I was here. So she said, so what do you want me to do? I said, please give me the cows. And I'll go into the fields. And I'll stay with the cows. And so, she said, OK. Reluctantly she said, OK. Go ahead, do it. And I was sitting. And I put on a kerchief, and I covered up my face, you know, to work as-- they shouldn't see that I am young. Because Germans were patrolling that village from all the sides. And I started to think so deep. And I said, gosh. What is going to happen to me? And I fainted again.

speaker: interviewee
text: And some people say that the cows are stupid animals, but the cow stepped on me, and stepped on me, to started to revive. And, in the meantime, the Germans noticed. They didn't know who I was. They gave me something to drink.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I got up. And I brought the cows back to-- to her. And I said, you can not chase me out. I don't have where go. What shall I do? And she said, she didn't want to chase me out, but she was afraid of her-- of her brother, you know? He was a drunkard. And he-- whenever he got drunk, he was not responsible. So many times when I was going horseback riding to-- to pick up the crops from the fields, you know, I saw a dilapidated house. It was probably-- this woman told me-- Francisca-- it was struck by lightning, and it burned.

speaker: interviewee
text: And one day, I tried to investigate that house. And I saw there was a big-- a deep basement. And I told her, I'm going to that basement, but give me some food. And from time to time, bring me food. And sure enough, in the basement, I was. Some people passed by, then a horse passing by, Germans were passing by. And I was there in that basement from the end of '43 til March, or April of 1944.

speaker: interviewee
text: And the city-- our city-- it is easy to say. And I thought that I am going to-- to-- to-- to go crazy, you know? So I used a recite Russian poems, and Polish poems, and German poems, just to keep my memory going, you know?

speaker: interviewee
text: And many times I felt like just to going out, and said, go ahead and kill me. But my mother's words, I could constantly hear in my ear. So I said no, I'm going to survive, I'm going to survive. And sure enough, I did survive. The Germans said that the Russians-- the Germans retreated. And the Russians took over. I find it out later. And later, the Germans came back, and the Russians retreated. And finally, in '44-- was it March or April-- I don't remember-- that the Russians took over that part. And it was Russia from my time. And then, I started to look. I didn't know where to go. I went to Nadia. And I came to Nadia. Nadia became a prostitute. Her husband never came back. I told you I forget to tell an episode-- if I may-- what happened once.

speaker: interviewer
text: Sure.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I was once in Nadia's-- at the attic. Once there was a knock, and a dog started to bark. And her husband came. And I said, oh. I was always at the attic. But this day, I had heightened fever, and I begged her. It was very cold. She should let me stay in the-- in the house. And she let me stay.

speaker: interviewee
text: And when they started to knock, I went under the bed, you know? And I was hiding under her bed. And sure enough-- and he came.

speaker: interviewee
text: We didn't seen each other. They made love, and I was laying under the bed-- and I-- underneath the bed. And I was just petrified. I thought that the-- the bed is going to collapse, and he'll discover me! And I didn't know who-- who-- what kind of human being-- if he is a-- if he is a mensch.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I was lucky. It really was meant I should survive that far. First of all, I was born in a-- I don't know how to call this in-- in English. There was a special word-- that I was born in a little bag-- like animals are born, you know? And my mother dried it out. And everybody said that this is a symbol of-- of luck, of luck, and that I will be lucky to-- maybe this-- I don't know what to believe anymore.

speaker: interviewee
text: And the dog-- and-- and he came into the house. So the children were frightened of the dog. So he let the dog out at the attic. And imagine if I would be there, I think the dog would-- would pull me apart. So in every little misery, there was a little luck.

speaker: interviewer
text: Let me go back for a moment, if I can. You talked about--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: --the time. For a time you stayed in a cave, which was formed by clay, I believe you said.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah?

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you tell me the time that you spent in that cave, and describe it for me as well?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, yeah. This was the time. And when I came back from Nadia's, it was '42.

speaker: interviewer
text: And for how long were you staying there?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't know. I really lost time, but I think that I stayed long. Later-- and I asked Nadia. And she said, you know, she said that she brought me six times food, and she didn't come more than once a week. And her sister said that she also came a few times. So, maybe-- maybe two months. I really don't know exactly.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you were by yourself?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, all by myself.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what-- can you describe the cave, please?

speaker: interviewee
text: The cave was not too deep, you know, because I couldn't take it to-- to-- through the day. But it was a cave. And nobody saw me. And--

speaker: interviewer
text: How-- how big was it?

speaker: interviewee
text: It wasn't too big. I-- I had to sit. When I walked out, I couldn't straighten out. I thought that I'll never be able to walk even, you know? My legs were swollen from poor circulation, from sitting. And I-- I didn't think that I'll be normal. And it's really a miracle-- a miracle that I am normal, you know? I'm normal, but if I can speak now, and tell everything, so-- doesn't really mean I'm crazy. [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewer
text: And during that time, you stayed in that cave the entire day?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, yeah, yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: So now, you were discussing-- you saw Nadia after the War?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. I came to Nadia. Nadia said, stay with me. And I didn't want to stay. And I thanked her. And I hugged her. And I kissed her. OK, she was-- she was mean, because of her nerves, you know? But because of her, I survived. I also went to this Polish woman, you know, where I worked. And she brought me food to the-- the dilapidated house. It wasn't a house anymore. And she said like this to me, she said-- she called me [NON-ENGLISH], because when she find me at the cemetery, I told her that my name is Tasha. And she said-- and she said like this-- how about staying with me, she said. She said that it's her God, the Christian God, she said, that saved me. I said, well, I don't know which God saved me. And she said, you know, as Polish citizens-- it was Russia at the time-- as Polish citizens, we had the right to leave Russia and go to Poland. And from Poland, they said, you know, that the Haganah is working. It will take us to Palestine.

speaker: interviewee
text: So, this Polish woman said, come with me to Poland. And then-- and you'll be with us. And I said, no. I have to find my people. And I remember I came and I saw this-- this Jewish-- this Jewish man, you know? And I begged him. I said, tell me something about-- maybe you know somebody from my family, maybe somebody survived. But he spoke to him Polish. He spoke to him Russian. And I spoke German. And I couldn't say one word in Jewish, you know? I tried to forget it. I didn't know to well Jewish, but the commies spoke Russian and Polish. Because I was afraid that wherever I'll be, I should not speak Jewish, you know?

speaker: interviewee
text: And-- and somehow I forgot. And that man-- spat at-- that old man had a cane. He-- he stomped his foot and-- and the cane. And he spat directly into my face. And he said, like this-- if a Jewish girl, after all that Russia-- what all those people did to us-- and you speak their language-- he said [NON-ENGLISH], meaning, your own language. And somehow I couldn't say one word. I cried bitterly. And then I worked again. And I met-- I met a father from my best girlfriend, Moishe Drucker. He was in the Russian army. And he-- he came back to Rovno. His girl-- or his daughter-- was my best friend. And each time he had to call my name, he called her name. And the man was going. And he will-- married a Jewish woman from Russia and had the baby with her already. And he said, stay with me. And I felt that I am not doing any justice to him, because I'm a constant reminder of his family. And, one day, I met a Jewish woman, very nice girl, and I asked if there were some Jewish young people. Maybe she can introduce me. Maybe I'll find out if my sister is alive. Maybe I would find out. And the next day, I met her. And she was with my husband, my current husband. And, you know, I felt very uncomfortable, because my husband started to ask me questions.

speaker: interviewee
text: He became very interested in me-- not in me as a person, I assume-- in my story, you know? And she got very jealous. And I wanted to go home. Home was where that friend, Drucker, you know, by my-- my girlfriend's father.

speaker: interviewee
text: And, she said that she has a headache. She wants to go home. And later on, he took her home. And we became acquainted. And I met a girl, you know, people started to come from old villages, from hiding, from churches. And I met a girlfriend who survived, came back from Russia. She was with her-- with her parents in Russia. She came back. And her parent and her aunt and uncle-- she was with an uncle and aunt, not her parents. And she said, you'll stay with us. And whatever will happen to Cilla, and my friend, it will happen to you too. And it happened that my-- Cilla, my friend, started to-- I kept company with this fellow, Nathan. And I kept company with Phillip. And we wanted to leave Russia and to go to Poland and from Poland to Palestine. But this-- this woman, the aunt of my-- my friend-- Levino is their name-- he said, if you want to go, you-- we'll marry you off, and you'll go. And sure enough, we got married.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I was really reluctant. My husband, was a nice guy, but I knew him only six weeks. And my parents had a doctor, Dr. Ehrlich, who survived. And I came to him. I was 20-years-old at the time. And I said, what shall I do?

speaker: interviewee
text: He said listen, I respected your parents very, very much. I will do anything for you. But you are only 20, but you're mature enough. And even if your-- your parents would be alive, they wouldn't be able to tell you what to do. You have to do what your instinct tells you. And-- and sure enough, we got married, and we came to Poland. And then from Poland, we were also smuggled through. The Haganah smuggled us through to Czechoslovakia, to Asch. And we came to Munich. And not far from Munich there was displace camp, Foehrenwald. And we came to Foehrenwald.

speaker: interviewee
text: And you know, there were many camps in Germany, because the Jews didn't have where to go. Yes, I forgot to tell. When I came back to Rovno, and I met some friends that I went together to school. So they said to me-- they started to cross themselves-- and said, you still alive? You still alive?

speaker: interviewee
text: And many of them said Hitler did not do a good job. This was the welcome. So we knew that we can not remain in Poland. And didn't know where to go,

speaker: interviewee
text: So the only way to-- the only place to go was Palestine. And that time they were called this. Because Israel, Palestine also and the English mandate. And they did not let the Jews in. But the Haganah smuggled us-- smuggled through.

speaker: interviewee
text: But-- and we were in Foehrenwald. And we got married. And you know, we used to meet and decide what we do. Some of us-- the majority of us-- were afraid to have children, because we were afraid another Holocaust surely come. And I decided again, we said, gosh. We should not give Hitler the satisfaction of ruining us altogether. And we said, no. We are going to have children. We wouldn't give him the satisfaction. And we-- I registered my husband to go to Palestine, but-- and I was pregnant. And I didn't tell the Haganah that I am pregnant. But-- and it showed already. They said, sorry, you cannot go. And if I would have gone, I would be in exodus-- the time in Cyprus. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And then, my son was born. And-- I don't know. They didn't let-- we couldn't go to-- to Palestine at the time. And Israel became already a free nation on May 14, 1948. And this was the day-- the year that my son got-- was born. He was born on February 3, 1948.

speaker: interviewee
text: And somehow, I was afraid already to go. I was afraid something should happen there to my son, you know? And then we decided that we will stay in the United States. And we applied for a visa, but it took us a long-- my husband worked-- worked for a joint distribution in Schleissheim. And through the joint distribution, we got an affidavit to go to the United States.

speaker: interviewee
text: And we came to the United States. We came by boat. It was the General Muir. It was a French war boat. And also-- and it was December, November. It was very stormy. We had to turn back for a few days, and we lost a few days with the voyage. Instead of taking seven or eight days, it took about 12 or 13 days. And we came to the United States. We came to Boston. And from Boston, the same night, 3:00 or 4:00 o'clock in the morning, we came to Manhattan-- to Broadway-- some place-- to a very cool hotel.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I remember that my son, he wasn't even 2-years-old, you now? On the boat he lost so much weight. He couldn't eat anything.

speaker: interviewee
text: And the captain always used to praise me, because everybody was seasick. My husband worked in the kitchen. He used to bring oranges, but I was too sick to eat.

speaker: interviewee
text: And on top of me there was another woman, a Polish woman. Women with children had cabins, you know? And we had those bunk beds. On top there was this Polish woman. She constantly regurgitated. And she also had a little baby. And she didn't fed the-- she didn't feed the baby. And I wanted to take her baby too to the dining room.

speaker: interviewee
text: And that French captain said at the time, the only brave woman that brings the child to the dining room. But anyway, he didn't eat anything. And when we came off the boat, he was a bundle of bones.

speaker: interviewee
text: And we came to that hotel. And there was this black woman came. And she brought us some food. And my son was very afraid. And he asked me why she has a rubber face you know? She thought, was she masked? Why is she wearing a mask?

speaker: interviewee
text: And the joint distribution gave us an apartment. And they would support us until my husband would get a job. But my husband said he doesn't want any charity. They should give him any job-- any job. So my husband started. He was a bookkeeper in Europe. He was a controller. He started to peel potatoes in yeshiva, you know, and to clean floors, and wash floors. And later on, he got another job. And he brought himself up. And we got another apartment.

speaker: interviewee
text: And my daughter was born. And things started to get better and better. And I started to go to school. And I learned-- yes-- when I was in Germany yet, I went to [NON-ENGLISH] University. You know? And I studied there.
